slides.title: Fast Network Notifications: problem statement, requirements and gap analysis
Problems and gap analysis for DCI congestion notification
Use cases and Requirement for Flow Control Collaboration Across DCNs and WAN
Ability requirements for stability guarantees in per-packet load balancing networks
A Framework for Fast Network Notifications

polls.text: Have you read or reviewed this document (or the one adopted in the RTGWG)?
Do you think this document forms a good basis for adoption?

chat.author: David Black
chat.text: Audio is ok

chat.author: Ketan Talaulikar
chat.text: Welcome to FANN

chat.author: Eddie Ruan
chat.text: Welcome to FANN

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: Hey Eddie. Would be nice to be able to see you  (if you're not in bed ;-)

chat.author: Jie Dong
chat.text: hope we can have some fun here:)

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: Hello :-)

chat.author: Eddie Ruan
chat.text: Hi Adrian, nice to meet you here

chat.author: Jeff Tantsura
chat.text: Hey Eddie :)

chat.author: Eddie Ruan
chat.text: Hi Jeff, just saw you walked in. :)

chat.author: Mike McBride
chat.text: What's the purpose of the problem statement, etc not being published.

chat.author: Alia Atlas
chat.text: hello

chat.author: Carlos Bernardos
chat.text: @Mike the purpose is to guide the framework work, and not publishing it is just stated in the charter

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: @Mike The IESG seems to be tidal on this sort of thing. Current thinking is along the lines of "What purpose would be served by publication?"

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: My advice. Work on the doc as though it was going to be an RFC. When it is ready, talk to the AD and get them to read the doc before saying yes or no

chat.author: Mike McBride
chat.text: Thanks will do. If problem/gap/requirement documents are typically not published I'll leave it alone.

chat.author: Ketan Talaulikar
chat.text: The document is identified as a support document for WG decision making and guiding the framework and further work. I leave it to the WG on how much time to spend on polishing it and when to switch focus on the framework.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: It's a bit peculiar that the criticism of BFD's time is less about what the protocol can carry (it carries timers in microseconds), and more about what implementations have chosen to do for scale.  Different applications might push the core protocol faster.

chat.author: Carlos Bernardos
chat.text: For online note taking this is the notepad: https://notes.ietf.org/notes-ietf-126-fann

chat.author: Ketan Talaulikar
chat.text: There is recognition that this is a fast moving area in the industry.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: Much of the recognition is there is a great deal of sticky pasta being thrown rapidly at the wall to see what clings to it.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: @meetecho The FANN room mics are running very quiet.

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: @Jeffrey Haas do you mean for local or remote attendees?

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: locally. Remote may be fine because I'm having to read the AI transcripts because I can't hear the speakers.

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: Ack, I'll let the AV team know

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: guys, I have a question on what document will establish the type of information of Fast Network Notifications, would this be established by the problem statement? Requirements doc further down?

chat.author: Carlos Bernardos
chat.text: the ps document should shed the light on what is needed (based on the gap analysis) and then the framework define and specify that

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: thanks Carlos, question for Jie, can you clarify what you mean by "Fine-grained network status information"? What would this entail and how this is limited to  link failures/ signal degradation ?

chat.author: Joel Halpern
chat.text: I note that pfc has an explicit lifetime, which could easily be shorter than the latency across the wide area network.  Is there an expectation that this propagation takes that into account?

chat.author: Mike McBride
chat.text: @Caroline: Look at section 4.1: "This can include quantifiable network metrics like link utilization, queue length,  level of congestion, link or node delay, jitter, packet loss, etc.

chat.author: Jie Dong
chat.text: @Carolina Caeiro as Mike replied, fine-grained network status information can be quantifiable information about network status  such as link utilization, link degradation, level of congestion or queue buildup information

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: thank you both -- looking at the draft now

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: @Joel. Important question, but akin to interflap or bandwidth variance in TE. I think trigger thresholds and damping will be critical

chat.author: Joel Halpern
chat.text: @Adrian I am concerned because the PFC generation is structured around assumptions about network topology, and thus it seems important in trying to propagate such information to recognize the differences in cases and explain how to deal with them.  For our existing IP TE solutions we recognize the inherent latency of the responses in the design of the system.

chat.author: Jie Dong
chat.text: Regarding the scope of recipients, my understanding is the major target is the network nodes which are in the traffic forwarding path and can take actions in the network layer, delivering the notification to hosts would need coordination with the transport area WGs.
